packs.source_post:
Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.

We're all-in on AI-native development. I wrote about how we restructured our workflow to make this safe — spec-first design, TDD as a hard constraint, multi-model review, security audits on every commit (link in comments).

That playbook is working. We're shipping faster than we ever have.

But it's surfacing a much harder question:

When AI writes most of the code, how do you know if your team is actually getting better?

Seriously. Think about it.

Lines of code — meaningless. PRs merged — inflated. Sprint velocity — noise. Even unit test coverage, the old gold standard, breaks down when AI generates hundreds of passing tests that don't verify anything meaningful.

Meanwhile the real risks are subtle:

→ Code turnover. Are AI-generated modules stable or constantly rewritten?
→ Quality attribution. Can you tell the difference between human-written and AI-assisted code in production?
→ False acceleration. Are you shipping faster, or generating more to fix later?
→ Test integrity. Do your tests actually catch failures, or just inflate coverage numbers?

Every engineering leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when things feel fast. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

We've been working on a framework for measuring developer productivity in the AI-first era. Not ready to share it yet. But I think this might be the most important unsolved problem in engineering leadership right now.

The teams that crack measurement will compound their advantage. The rest will ship a lot of code and wonder why nothing changed.

source_dissection.psychological_move: Specific-number cost reveal reframed as organizational absurdity — the 'expensive tool replacing headcount' paradox. The opener weaponizes a concrete spend figure to create cognitive dissonance: AI was supposed to save money, but the bill alone equals a human salary. This triggers the 'wait, what?' reflex that makes the reader need resolution.
source_dissection.narrative_arc: escalation-with-pivot: success signal (spending = commitment) → success validation (shipping faster) → contradiction reveal (the success metric itself is broken) → diagnostic paralysis (nothing measures what matters) → unresolved tension (we're working on it, but it's hard)
source_dissection.opener_mechanic_description: specific_number + reframe: concrete cost anchor → organizational absurdity comparison (headcount territory) → team-size escalation (under 10, then 'even smaller' backend team). The mechanic is: make a number feel viscerally wrong by comparing it to a familiar human-scale reference point.
source_dissection.source_opener_text:
Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.

hook_mechanics.sentence: Our Claude Code API bill just crossed $18,000/month.
hook_mechanics.structural_function: specificity bomb — concrete number with time frame creates material reality
hook_mechanics.psychological_effect: The '$18,000' stops scrolls because it's specific enough to feel leaked, not marketed. 'Just crossed' implies real-time tracking, not retrospective bragging. Reader thinks: 'That's either a huge waste or a huge signal — which is it?'

hook_mechanics.sentence: That's headcount territory. For a team of under 10 engineers. And most of that spend came from our backend team alone — which is even smaller.
hook_mechanics.structural_function: reframe + escalation — converts abstract cost into human-resource equivalent, then shrinks the denominator to intensify absurdity
hook_mechanics.psychological_effect: 'Headcount territory' reframes spend as replacement logic (AI was supposed to reduce this). 'Under 10' → 'even smaller' progressively narrows scope, making the per-person burn rate feel increasingly irrational. Reader must resolve: is this success or failure?

source_dissection.body_structure: progressive revelation through staged contradiction: success claim → pivot question → diagnostic list of broken metrics → deeper risk taxonomy (bulleted) → future promise withheld. The body systematically destroys every comfortable measurement, then offers a framework that doesn't exist yet.
source_dissection.ending_type: terminal verdict with competitive stakes — closes by bifurcating outcomes (compound advantage vs. ship-and-wonder) to create urgency and identity threat
source_dissection.closer_text: The teams that crack measurement will compound their advantage. The rest will ship a lot of code and wonder why nothing changed.
source_dissection.mirrorability_reason: The '$X/month = headcount territory' structure is fully portable across any domain where tool spend replaces human roles. The 'specific cost → human equivalent → team size shrink' pattern works for: software subscriptions, contractor spend, cloud infrastructure, agency fees, any recurring cost. 'Claude Code API' is content; the engine is cost-to-headcount comparison.
source_dissection.register_energy: founder-confessional with controlled anxiety — the voice of someone who has good news and bad news, sharing both to demonstrate clear-eyed leadership. Not panic, not triumph; the tension of 'this is working AND this is broken.' The lowercase, casual contractions, and direct address ('Seriously. Think about it.') create intimacy that makes the intellectual content feel like a sidebar at a conference, not a manifesto.
source_dissection.virality_reason: The opener works universally because everyone has experienced 'this tool costs more than the person it replaced' — the SaaS sprawl anxiety is cross-industry, and the specific number makes it feel like insider intelligence rather than thought leadership.

macro.reason: Any professional understands 'bill crossed $X/month' and 'headcount territory.' No domain knowledge required — 'Claude Code' is a product name but functions grammatically as any tool; the sentence works even if reader doesn't know what it is.

fast.reason: '$18,000' lands at word 4 (Our-Claude-Code-$18,000). The '$' symbol is the payload — specific, surprising, concrete. Passes by word 4.

simple.reason: Every word is conversational: 'Our,' 'bill,' 'crossed,' 'month,' 'headcount,' 'territory,' 'team,' 'under,' 'engineers,' 'spend,' 'came,' 'from,' 'backend,' 'alone,' 'smaller.' No jargon, no corporate speak, no press release language.

That's headcount territory. For a team of under 8 revenue-facing people. And most of that spend came from our presales team alone — which is even smaller.

Meanwhile the real risks are subtle:

→ Context turnover. Are AI-generated discovery notes stable or constantly rewritten by the next SE who touches the account?
→ Quality attribution. Can you tell the difference between human-built and AI-assisted deal strategy in the forecast?
→ False acceleration. Are you cycling faster, or generating more to repair in procurement?
→ POV integrity. Does your point of view actually change the buyer's thinking, or just inflate confidence scores?

Every revenue leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when activity feels high. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

The teams that crack measurement will compound their advantage. The rest will generate a lot of pipeline and wonder why nothing closes.

Every revenue leader adopting AI tools will hit this wall. The spend is easy to justify when cycles feel fast. It's much harder to prove the investment is actually working.

packs.source_post:
We had five design leads in three years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same - the next hire will fix it. Better portfolio, stronger process, more senior title.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

In most cases the design team wasn't the real problem. The structure around the design team was the problem. You can hire the best designer in the world and put them downstream of every decision that matters. You give them no real authority, involve them once the direction is already locked and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. Design let this happen too. We trained designers to execute inside systems, not shape them, to improve the brief, not question whether the brief was right. 

The designers who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

source_dissection.psychological_move: Pattern observation with systemic reframe: establishes a recurring failure cycle, then immediately undermines the reader's assumed explanation (talent) with a hidden structural cause. The hook weaponizes the reader's own likely experience—having blamed hiring failures on the hire—to make them feel complicit in misdiagnosis.
source_dissection.opener_mechanic_description: pattern observation: repeated failure with identical outcome → implicit diagnostic trap (reader assumes talent problem) → structural reframe that inverts the causal arrow
source_dissection.source_opener_text: We had five design leads in three years. Same result every time.

hook_mechanics.sentence: We had five design leads in three years.
hook_mechanics.psychological_effect: The exact numbers signal lived experience, not theory. 'Five' and 'three' create a rhythm of failure—enough to be a pattern, not a one-off. The reader's brain immediately starts diagnosing: bad hires? bad manager? toxic culture? This diagnostic urge IS the hook—they're already engaged in the puzzle.

The repetition ('every time') hammers the pattern while deliberately withholding WHAT the result is. The reader must continue to resolve the gap. More critically, it primes the reader to expect a talent explanation—making the eventual structural reframe land harder as a surprise.

source_dissection.body_structure: progressive revelation with dual-ownership twist: starts with org's blind spot (hiring as solution), reveals structural cause (downstream authority), then pivots to industry's uncomfortable complicity (designers trained to execute not shape), closes with mutual responsibility
source_dissection.ending_type: unresolved tension with mutual challenge—cycle continues until both sides own their part
source_dissection.closer_text: It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.
source_dissection.mirrorability_reason: The 'X roles in Y time, same result' structure is domain-agnostic. Works for sales managers, engineering leads, CMOs, any recurring hire-and-fire pattern. The numbers are adjustable; the structural move (pattern → assumed talent fix → hidden systemic cause) transfers to any function.
source_dissection.register_energy: measured exasperation, institutional fatigue—quiet authority of someone who's watched the same mistake repeat. Not rant; weary precision. The lowercase and minimal punctuation in the source amplify this 'too tired to perform corporate enthusiasm' register.
source_dissection.virality_reason: The opener triggers every manager's secret fear: that their hiring failures might be their own system's fault, not bad luck with candidates. The numbers feel like evidence; the withheld result creates need for closure.

macro.reason: Any professional understands 'had five [role] in three years, same result.' No domain jargon. 'Design leads' is the only role-specific term, and it's immediately parseable as 'senior hire who didn't work out.'

fast.reason: 'five' lands at word 3—the specific number that makes the claim concrete and alarming. 'Five design leads' is the payload; everything before is just setup.

simple.reason: Every word is conversational. A 16-year-old understands 'had,' 'five,' 'same,' 'result.' Zero corporate jargon, zero press-release language.

posts.text:
We had five SE leads in three years. Same result every time.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next hire will fix it. Better demo skills, deeper product knowledge, more enterprise polish.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

In most cases the SE team wasn't the real problem. The structure around the SE team was the problem. You can hire the sharpest technical seller in the world and introduce them as "technical resource" in the first ten seconds. You give them no real authority in the deal, bring them in once the narrative is already locked, and the outcome is predictable.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

But here's the part the industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained solution engineers to execute inside systems, not shape them — to improve the demo, not question whether the demo was the right move. I've watched it on dozens of calls. The AE owns the room. The SE waits for their cue. The buyer makes their judgment before the SE ever speaks.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

posts.label: B3
posts.text:
They've already filed this person under 'supporting actor.'

I see it on every enterprise call. The SE gets introduced as 'our technical resource' and the buyer's shoulders drop. Phone comes out. The frame is set before a single feature gets shown.

I've watched it on a hundred enterprise calls. The SE gets introduced as 'our technical resource' or 'here to walk through the product.' Every time, I see the buyer's posture shift. Shoulders drop. Phone comes out. They've already filed this person under 'supporting actor.'

The company thinks it hired wrong. Better resume, bigger brand, more years. Same result six months later.

But nobody audits the introduction. Nobody tracks what happens in the ten seconds after 'let me bring in my SE.' The frame is set before a single feature gets shown. 'Technical resource' means 'not strategic.' 'Here to demo' means 'not here to challenge my thinking.'

And here's what the industry won't say out loud. SEs let this happen too.

We trained a generation to wait for the brief, polish the deck, never push back on the deal's actual shape. The SEs who actually steer strategy didn't get promoted into that seat. They walked in and took it. Refused the frame. Changed the room's temperature before the first slide.

Two failures. Same cycle. Both sides waiting for the other to blink first.

posts.label: B4
posts.text:
I watched an SE get introduced as 'our technical resource' in a $400K deal last quarter. Same introduction I'd heard twenty times before. Same result every time.

The buyer's shoulders dropped three inches. You could see it. The meeting became a feature walkthrough. The SE, who'd later architect the entire deployment scope, sat there answering questions about API limits while the real objections went unspoken.

Every time, the assumption is the same — the next SE will fix it. Better demo skills, deeper product knowledge, more certifications.

Nobody asks why the role keeps failing.

In most cases the SE wasn't the real problem. The structure around the SE was the problem. You can hire the sharpest technical mind in the industry and slot them downstream of every strategic decision that matters. You give them no authority to qualify or push back, involve them once the deal narrative is already locked and the bad fit is already invisible.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

BUT here's the part our industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained them to execute inside systems, not shape them — to improve the demo, not question whether the demo should happen at all.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome, and until both sides own their part of it, the cycle just continues.

posts.label: B5
posts.text:
You bring them in after the AE has already promised the moon and the buyer has already decided what they want to hear. Then you wonder why the 'technical resource' can't save the deal.

Every time, the fix is the same — better deck, sharper demo, more senior title. Nobody asks why the introduction keeps failing.

In most cases the SE wasn't the real problem. The structure around the SE was the problem. You can hire the sharpest technical mind in the industry and give them no context before the call, no seat during strategy, no authority to push back on scope. You bring them in after the AE has already promised the moon and the buyer has already decided what they want to hear.

The org calls it a talent problem and hires again.

BUT here's the part our industry is less comfortable saying. SEs let this happen too. We trained them to demo inside briefs, not question whether the brief was right. To improve the deck, not challenge whether the deck should exist. To be the most prepared person in a room where they have the least power.

The SEs who do have real influence got there by refusing to accept the seat they were given and going to find a better one themselves.

It's two failures leading to the same outcome. And until both sides own their part, the cycle just continues.
